steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student wants to present biographical analysis to an audience that has never heard of it. The primary goal is to make the concept clear — that means a definition must come first, before examples or comparisons.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'An approach to art criticism, biographical analysis considers the personal experiences of the artist.': Opens by situating the concept (art criticism), then names and defines it. This is exactly how you introduce an unfamiliar term to a new audience. ✓
  • Choice B — 'A stylistic analysis of Water Lilies might consider how the painting's loose brushwork represents an impressionist style.': Describes stylistic analysis, not biographical analysis. The target concept is never mentioned. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Stylistic analysis differs from biographical analysis in that stylistic analysis examines how an artwork's visual features contribute to its overall style.': Contrasts the two approaches without ever defining biographical analysis. A reader unfamiliar with it still won't know what it is after reading this. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Women in the Garden's portrayal of a figure holding red and white roses alludes to Monet's romantic difficulties.': Gives only an example — no definition or even mention of what approach this illustrates. Useless as an introduction. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
